title: Case on Impeachment of Chairperson of Audit and Inspection Board
text:
1Case on Impeachment of Chairperson of Audit and Inspection Board 
[2024Hun-Na2, March 13, 2025]
* First Draft
In this case, the Court rejected the claim for removal from office sought in the petition for 
an impeachment trial. This petition alleged that Respondent severely violated the Constitution 
and statutes by ⅰ) undermining the independence of the Board of Audit and Inspection 
(hereinafter referred to as the “BAI”), ⅱ) conducting targeted inspections against the 
Chairperson of the Anti-Corruption and Civil Rights Commission and others, ⅲ) breaching 
duties as the Chairperson of the BAI, and ⅳ) refusing to comply with the National 
Assembly’s request for data submission and to allow its perusal of a record. 
Background of the Case
Respondent was appointed as Chairperson of the BAI on November 12, 2021. On 
December 2, 2024, 170 members of the National Assembly proposed an impeachment 
resolution against Respondent on the ground that he had violated the Constitution or statutes 
in the performance of his official duties. On December 5, 2024, the National Assembly 
passed the impeachment resolution at a plenary session with the approval of 188 of the 300 
members. On the same day, the impeachment prosecutor submitted the original copy of the 
impeachment resolution to the Court and filed the instant petition for an impeachment trial.
Subject Matter of Review
The subject matter of review in this case is whether Chairperson Choe Jaehae of the BAI 
violated the Constitution or statutes in the course of performing his official duties and 
whether a decision to remove him from office should be rendered.
Summary of the Decision
1. Assessment of the impairment of independence of the BAI
Respondent’s remarks in a plenary session of the Legislation and Judiciary Committee of 
the National Assembly and during National Assembly oversight, to the effect that “the BAI is 
2supporting the President’s governance,” can be interpreted as meaning that smooth 
governance may be facilitated through diligent audits and inspections. It is therefore difficult 
to conclude that such remarks have the potential either to impair the independence of the BAI 
or to undermine the citizens’ trust in its audit and inspection functions. Consequently, they do 
not violate Article 2, Section (1) of the Board of Audit and Inspection Act (hereinafter 
referred to as the “BAI Act”) or Article 63 of the State Public Officials Act.
Respondent’s amendment of the directive of the BAI to add a provision granting the Prime 
Minister the authority to request audits and inspections cannot be characterized as violating 
Article 23 of the BAI Act, which provides that in the case of optional accounting audits, an 
audit may be conducted upon the request of the Prime Minister. Conferring upon the Prime 
Minister the authority to request audits and inspections for the public interest cannot be 
characterized as depriving the BAI of its independence in the performance of its official duty 
or its political neutrality, and therefore cannot be said to run counter to, inter alia, Article 2, 
Section (1) of the BAI Act or Article 100 of the Constitution. 
Respondent also requested an investigation into the former Director of the National 
Security Office and others. Under Article 35 of the BAI Act, where a crime is found to be 
suspected as a result of an inspection, the Board of Audit and Inspection shall bring an 
accusation to the investigative authorities. Therefore, this request for investigation cannot be 
regarded as a loss of political neutrality in violation of Article 2, Section (1) of the BAI Act 
or Article 56 of the State Public Officials Act.
2. Assessment of the inspections against the Chairperson of the Anti-Corruption and 
Civil Rights Commission and others 
An inspection of the Chairperson and other public officials of the Anti-Corruption and 
Civil Rights Commission, conducted to rectify their unlawful or improper acts, is within the 
scope of the BAI’s authority to conduct inspections of official duties. In addition, the work 
discipline inspection of the Chairperson and others, carried out on the basis of numerous 
reports, was a specific-matter inspection. Thus, both inspections cannot be regarded as 
violating, inter alia, Article 2, Section (1) of the BAI Act or Article 56 of the State Public 
Officials Act. 
3However, in the course of implementing the report on the above work discipline inspection, 
Respondent caused the computer department to alter the electronic document system so that 
the report could be executed without perusal by the audit and inspection commissioner in 
charge of the case. Under the BAI directive on the handling of audit affairs, where the Audit 
and Inspection Council of Commissioners decided to modify and implement the corrective 
action plan for inspection results, the results must be executed after perusal by the 
commissioner in charge of the case. This directive grants the commissioner in charge of the 
case substantive authority to peruse and approve, which cannot be substituted by the perusal 
and approval of the Chairperson of the BAI or others. Therefore, Respondent exceeded the 
scope of legally authorized powers and infringed the commissioner in charge of the case’s 
authority of perusal, thereby violating Article 56 of the State Public Officials Act. However, 
the Court does not find that it has been proven beyond a reasonable doubt that Respondent 
acted with intent to abuse authority, and since the perusal by the commissioner in charge of 
the case still remains pending at the approval-request stage and the approval date has been 
left blank, Respondent’s conduct cannot be characterized as the entry of false information. 
Therefore, the conduct does not constitute abuse of authority under Article 123 of the 
Criminal Act or falsification or alteration of public electromagnetic records under Article 
227-2 of the Criminal Act.
3. Assessment of the breach of duties as the Chairperson of the BAI 
With respect to the audit and inspection concerning the relocation of the presidential office 
and residence, Respondent did not record any false facts in the audit and inspection report, 
nor are there circumstances indicating a deficient audit and inspection. Therefore, Respondent 
cannot be regarded as having violated, inter alia, Article 2, Section (1) of the BAI Act or 
Article 56 of the State Public Officials Act.
With respect to the audit and inspection of the incident involving the shooting of a public 
official in the West Sea, there is no material indicating that the portion of the press release 
distributed by the BAI, as alleged by Claimant, constitutes military classified information of 
Grade I. Thus, the Court cannot find that Respondent violated, inter alia, Article 13, Section 
(1) of the Military Secret Protection Act.
4. Assessment of the refusal to comply with the National Assembly’s request for data 
4submission and the refusal to allow perusal of a record during an on-site inspection
Respondent refused to comply with the request of the Legislation and Judiciary Committee 
for data submission. However, it is difficult to find that the request was made in accordance 
with the procedures prescribed by the National Assembly Act and the Act on Testimony by 
Lay and Expert Witnesses before the National Assembly. Therefore, Respondent’s refusal to 
comply with the request cannot be regarded as constituting a violation of the Act on 
Testimony by Lay and Expert Witnesses before the National Assembly.
Respondent also refused to allow perusal during the on-site inspection. The notification of 
the on-site inspection was served in compliance with the procedures prescribed by the Act on 
Testimony by Lay and Expert Witnesses before the National Assembly, and there is no 
material indicating that any showing was made as to the statutory grounds for refusing the 
inspection under this Act. Therefore, the refusal to allow perusal constitutes a violation of 
Article 10, Section (4) and Article 4, Section (1) of the Act on Testimony by Lay and Expert 
Witnesses before the National Assembly. Nevertheless, such conduct does not constitute the 
crime of abandonment of duties under Article 122 of the Criminal Act or the crimes of abuse 
of authority and interference with the exercise of rights under Article 123 of the Criminal Act.
5. Whether Respondent should be removed from office 
In determining whether the Chairperson of the BAI’s violations of law constitute “grave 
violations of law” sufficient to warrant removal from office, consideration must be given , 
inter alia, to both the extent of the harm such violations cause to the constitutional order and 
the constitutional position of the Chairperson of the BAI, whose status is guaranteed to 
ensure the independent exercise of audit and inspection functions.
Respondent violated Article 56 of the State Public Officials Act by altering the electronic 
document system of the BAI so as to allow an audit and inspection report to be executed 
without perusal by the commissioner in charge of the case. However, there is room to view 
this as having been intended to facilitate the smooth implementation of audit and inspection 
results, and it is difficult to conclude that Respondent acted with a specific intent to interfere 
with or improperly influence those results.
Respondent also violated provisions of the Act on Testimony by Lay and Expert Witnesses 
5before the National Assembly by refusing to allow perusal of the minutes of the Audit and 
Inspection Council of Commissioners. Nonetheless, under the “Rule on the Operation, etc. of 
the Audit and Inspection Council of Commissioners,” meetings of the Council are required to 
be closed to the public, a rule that appears intended to secure the independence and political 
neutrality of the BAI. Further, at the time of the on-site inspection, the audit and inspection 
report, inter alia, was disclosed, and the commissioners who had participated in the Council 
meeting attended as witnesses. In light of these circumstances, inter alia, it is difficult to 
conclude that Respondent violated the statutes with an affirmative intent to disregard or 
contravene the legal order.
In sum, although some of Respondent’s acts in the performance of official duties violated 
the Constitution and statutes, it cannot be concluded that the negative impact of such 
violations on the constitutional order is of such gravity as to warrant depriving Respondent of 
the trust of the people indirectly vested in him. Therefore, no ground exists to justify the 
removal of Respondent from office.
Summary of Concurring Opinion of Three Justices
1. Whether the amendment of the directive at issue violates Article 2, Section (1) and 
Article 23 of the BAI Act
Respondent, by granting the Prime Minister —who assists the President —the authority to 
request an audit and inspection through the amendment of the directive at issue, compromised 
the independence and neutrality of the BAI, thereby violating Article 2, Section (1) of the 
BAI Act. Furthermore, the Prime Minister’s authority to request an audit and inspection 
under Article 23 of the BAI Act must reasonably be interpreted as being limited exclusively 
to the optional audit matters specified in that provision. Therefore, the amendment of the 
directive at issue also violates Article 23 of the BAI Act, which grants the Prime Minister the 
authority to request an audit and inspection solely with respect to such optional audit matters.
2. Whether the amendment of the directive at issue violates Article 100 of the 
Constitution and Article 12, Section (1), Item 1 of the BAI Act 
6Article 100 of the Constitution provides that matters concerning, inter alia, the “scope of 
official duties of the Board of Audit and Inspection” shall be prescribed by statute. Granting 
the Prime Minister the authority to request an audit and inspection constitutes a substantive 
alteration of the scope of the BAI’s official duties and therefore must be effected through a 
statutory amendment. Further, Article 12, Section (1), Item 1 of the BAI Act requires that 
matters concerning the “audit and inspection policies of the Board of Audit and Inspection” 
be decided by the Audit and Inspection Council of Commissioners. Since granting the Prime 
Minister the authority to request an audit and inspection can be regarded as a change in audit 
and inspection policy that significantly affects the objects or scope of audit and inspection, 
such a change must be decided by the Audit and Inspection Council of Commissioners. 
Nevertheless, Respondent amended the directive at issue without undergoing either the 
statutory amendment procedure or the decision-making procedure of the Audit and Inspection 
Council of Commissioners, thereby violating Article 100 of the Constitution and Article 12, 
Section (1), Item 1 of the BAI Act.
3. Whether the amendment of the directive at issue violates Article 56 of the State 
Public Officials Act 
Respondent, through the amendment of the directive at issue, created a channel through 
which the executive branch could interfere with the independent functions of the BAI and 
opened the possibility of mobilizing the BAI to conduct audits and inspections for political 
purposes. This undermines the independence and political neutrality of the BAI in the 
performance of its official duties and results in consequences contrary to the realization of the 
public interest for the entire citizenry, thereby constituting a violation of Article 56 of the 
State Public Officials Act.
4. Whether Respondent should be removed from office 
The alteration of the electronic document system and the refusal to grant access to meeting 
minutes, taken alone, cannot readily be regarded as constituting grave violations of law. 
Moreover, it is difficult to conclude that, at the time the directive at issue was amended, 
Respondent harbored an affirmative intent to impair the independence of the BAI, and no 
7request for an audit or inspection has in fact been made by the Prime Minister since that 
amendment. Even when the above constitutional and statutory violations are considered in 
their entirety, it is difficult to recognize that Respondent’s violations rise to the level of 
betraying the trust of the people —conferred indirectly through the appointing authority —to 
such an extent as to warrant the deprivation of that trust. Therefore, no ground exists to 
justify the decision to remove Respondent from office.
* This translation is provisional and subject to revision.
